author: @user-sf9mh1ms4q
text: It really did work, and really well too. It only takes a seconds to get it red hot staples on my DIY 18650 tab welder from microwave transformer.  I used 2 brass bolts, drilled 1mm hole in the middle (1/4" deep), used a 1/4" copper wire, used a hex crimper to attached it on both the bolt and the  cable. With a little solder, to finished the cable wires since to booster cable wire is a bit bigger than the tubing. Putting the bolt on a drill on to a grinding wheel made it easy to shrink it to ID of the copper tube.A shrink tubing to finish and make it look pro. Depending on the plastic, I press it for a second before inserting it on plastic. Need to blow on it the cool the wires faster though. Thanks again, it was fun and quick making it.

author: @user-sf9mh1ms4q

author: @valeriechaumeziere377
text:
Hello, Thank you for your excellent videos. 
Ref your plastic repair gun. I was canoeing in the South of France on the Ardeche river and one day a guy arrived to do some repairs on the canoes which were made of a hard orange coloured plastic. The canoes had all sorts of cuts and scrapes and holes in them. Obviously, this plastic (I suppose all plastics), melts at a certain given temperature. The repair man had several sticks of the same coloured plastic and he literally used a Hot Air gun (a sort of very hot hair dryer thingy) which he used to soften the area he wished to repair and the stick he was going to fill the damage with. Once soft enough, he bonded the soft plastic into the cut or hole and then used a metal scraper to smooth the repair out. Once it was cold again, he used fine sandpaper to finish the job. 
The Ardeche has some really serious rapids and the canoes could get quite badly damaged. We actually managed to bend a two man canoe completely in half, almost drowning me in the process!  
Thanks for all the very interesting vids.

text: A wonderful idea for bulky repairs!  I'll have to keep this in mind.  Of course, it would not be suitable for the tiny repairs I've done in the past.  Like inserting a quarter-inch pin to repair a latch.  The pin was made from a bit of paper clip, one end sanded.  I just heated the pin with a small torch or lighter while holding with very small pliers (needle nose).  The sanded pin helps get a better grip in the plastic (I did not want to have to repeat the "surgery" at a later date).

author: @Zodliness
text: @Robert Murray-Smith. It's handy to wind a few more turns on the new secondary side, adding taps it at various intervals, to create selectable outputs, it's better for applying staples to thicker materials like polycarbonate sheets. When you melt your staples into the crack, try rejoining it with the staple running parallel to the crack, not across it, your stapling method only supports the crack with one wire strand per staple. Make the undulations in your staples smaller, for tighter looking joins after sanding. Thanks for sharing.

Another innovative video,  thank you.  I have collected old microwave ovens for years. 
As a high voltage enthusiast, the transformers form the basis of of many projects that interest me. 
I have also done the high current stuff as well, but I go to great lengths to keep the secondary winding intact. The microwave ovens also can yield a digital clock and a very useful timer circuit.

author: @VinoVeritas_
text: Hi Rob. If possible, I would appreciate it if you could take another luck at your Al-Air battery. I am wondering how useful it could be as a primary battery for energy in winter. I bought a roll of aluminium foil yesterday for 65p (290mm X 20m) and it got me thinking how useful it would be to power appliances in the dark winter months when solar PV just doesn't provide the amount of energy required.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: will do mate - but what about the thing i did on the magnesium battery - that was pretty awesome even if i say so myself

replies.author: @VinoVeritas_
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering It was Rob, but the ubiquity of aluminium foil makes it more accessible. I was just wondering what a 290mm wide, 20m long carbon felt and aluminium jelly roll air battery could produce in terms of power? I made a choline chloride/urea/CMC gel a couple of years ago and it was producing a steady ~1v output which I coupled with a homemade joule thief to power an LED. However, it would be great seeing it doing some real work either powering a decent sized DC motor or a conductive ink heat panel.

replies.author: @williamarmstrong7199
replies.text: @VinoVeritas_ i doubt the power produced from a few grams of aluminium foil will do much. But a 5Kg Magnesium alloy wheel will produce oodles of power. Possibly KWh's worth.   5 or 6 of them in large buckets wired in series connected to a 250 volt inverter and away you go. Enough decent power to keep you going for ages. Scrap wheels (make sure they are magnesium) cost a few £ each though.

replies.author: @VinoVeritas_
replies.text: @williamarmstrong7199 The theoretical energy density of an Aluminium-Air Battery is 8kWh per kg and Magnesium-Air is 6.5kWh per kg. Aluminium is also half the price of magnesium. I can walk into any supermarket or convenience store and purchase a roll of aluminium foil. The same can't be said for magnesium.
